text: The diaphragm is open circumferentially close to its insertion on the costal margin.
The incision is made away from the central tendon.
The absence of the right lobe of the liver facilitates the exposure.
Once the diaphragm is opened, using the CT scan as a roadmap, the lesions are identified.
We use visual and palpation clues to localize all lesions visible on the CAT scan.
No resection is attempted until all lesions are identified and tattooed.
For the tattoo, we use bovie to cauterize the visceral pleura, followed by blue dye to tattoo the area.
Then using serial applications of the Echelon 60 on a green load, the lesions are amputated.
Here we are removing two lesions with one wedge resection of the right lower lobe.
This is the first specimen.
The two lesions are included in the specimen.
The final pathology will report the margin from the resected staple line.
We look for a margin of at least equal to the diameter of the lesion.
Here we are going after the two additional lesions in the right lower lobe.
This particular one appears to be a subplural lymph node, but it will be resected in order to remove confusion and future readings of the CAT scan.
Again, we use the Echelon 60 on a green load.

This technique allows for easy access to all lesions situated within two centimeters from the surface of the lung, regardless of their location in the lobes, and the procedure can be performed on the right, left, or combined safely.
For this last one, the margin is less important as it consists of a benign lymph node.
A drain is left inside the pleural cavity and we use a Blake 19 for that purpose.
Finally, the diaphragm is closed in one layer using a running continuous suture of No.
1 Proline.
The drain is removed within 48 hours.
The insertion site of the drain in the diaphragm was also reduced using an interrupted suture of 3-O Vicryl.
The other option is to pass the drain through the diaphragmatic opening, but care must be taken not to grasp the drain with the suture.
The drain is attached to an underwater seal system and kept to suction for the first 24 hours.
